title: Principles of relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics: a class of exactly solvable models
abstract: A system of interacting atoms is represented as an union of two subsystems, one of which is the system of atoms, and the other is an auxiliary scalar covariant field, which is equivalent to a given static interatomic potential of general form only in the non-relativistic approximation. It is shown that the auxiliary field is a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields, the parameters of which are related to singular points of the Fourier transform of the corresponding interatomic potential. The general form of the relativistic Hamiltonian system of interacting atoms is established. It is shown that the exact calculation of the relativistic partition function of a system of interacting atoms, taking into account the field degrees of freedom, reduces to renormalizing the parameters of the auxiliary field. It is established that the field degrees of freedom lead to a divergence in the total energy of a classical relativistic system - an analogue of the ultraviolet catastrophe. Quantization of the auxiliary field eliminates this divergence. The existence of a phase transition within the framework of relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics has been proven.
summary: This paper develops a theory for the relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics of interacting atoms. By representing interatomic potentials as an auxiliary scalar field, the author transforms a complex many-body problem into an exactly solvable model of an ideal gas of quasiparticles. The work demonstrates that quantizing this auxiliary field prevents the classical ultraviolet catastrophe and proves the existence of a phase transition at a specific critical temperature.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of a microscopic mechanism for irreversibility in classical mechanics and the difficulty of finding exactly solvable 3D models for the relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics of interacting atoms.
main_result: The author establishes a relativistic framework where interatomic interactions are represented by an auxiliary field, demonstrating that quantization resolves the classical ultraviolet catastrophe and that a phase transition occurs at a critical temperature.
method: The study uses a relativistic field theory approach, employing Fourier transformations, Legendre transformations, and the quadratic approximation of the Hamiltonian to reduce the complex partition function to a solvable form.
model_or_system: A relativistic system consisting of two subsystems: a collection of interacting atoms and an auxiliary scalar covariant field (a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields) that mediates the interatomic potential.
key_observables: Total energy, average energy of the field modes, heat capacity, and the dispersion law of the effective field.
important_parameters: Critical temperature (T_crit), particle density (n), field mass parameters (mu_s), coupling constants (gamma_s, kappa_s), and the high-temperature/low-temperature regimes.
main_assumptions: The model assumes atoms do not undergo destruction (valid for v << c), the interatomic potential is representable via simple poles in its Fourier transform, and the field is treated as a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields.
figures_summary: The figures show the domain of definition and behavior of a function f(T/T_s) for different coupling parameters and its behavior in the low-temperature region.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the foundational problem of irreversibility, introduces the relativistic atom-field model and its Hamiltonian, applies mathematical transformations to solve the partition function, analyzes the thermodynamic properties (energy, heat capacity), and concludes with the identification of a phase transition.
why_interesting: It provides a rare example of an exactly solvable 3D relativistic many-body model and offers a physical mechanism for irreversibility through field-induced 'lag' in interactions, which is relevant to the study of open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
